transitiontrees on the trajectories data

One analysis, its default output, and how to read it — nothing more

transitiontrees

2026-06-05

This is a plain walk-through of transitiontrees on the built-in trajectories data. Every step is a single default call — no subsetting, no tuning, no clever code. Each section is one analysis, its default output or plot, and a short reading.

The data

data(trajectories)            # bundled with transitiontrees
dim(trajectories)
#> [1] 138  15
head(trajectories)
#>      1         2            3            4            5            6           
#> [1,] "Active"  "Disengaged" "Disengaged" "Disengaged" "Active"     "Active"    
#> [2,] "Average" "Average"    "Average"    "Average"    "Average"    "Average"   
#> [3,] "Average" "Active"     "Active"     "Active"     "Active"     "Active"    
#> [4,] "Active"  "Active"     "Active"     "Active"     "Active"     "Average"   
#> [5,] "Active"  "Active"     "Active"     "Average"    "Active"     "Average"   
#> [6,] "Average" "Average"    "Disengaged" "Average"    "Disengaged" "Disengaged"
#>      7         8         9         10           11        12          
#> [1,] "Active"  "Active"  "Average" "Active"     "Average" "Active"    
#> [2,] "Average" "Average" "Average" "Active"     "Average" "Average"   
#> [3,] "Active"  "Active"  "Active"  "Average"    "Active"  "Active"    
#> [4,] "Average" "Active"  "Active"  "Active"     "Average" "Active"    
#> [5,] "Active"  "Active"  "Active"  "Disengaged" "Active"  "Active"    
#> [6,] "Average" "Average" "Average" "Disengaged" "Average" "Disengaged"
#>      13        14        15       
#> [1,] NA        NA        NA       
#> [2,] "Average" "Average" "Average"
#> [3,] "Active"  "Active"  "Active" 
#> [4,] "Average" "Active"  "Average"
#> [5,] "Active"  "Average" "Average"
#> [6,] "Average" "Average" "Average"

One row per learner, one column per time-step, three engagement states (Active, Average, Disengaged). Trailing NAs mark the end of a trajectory. This wide frame is exactly what context_tree() consumes.

Fit a context tree

tree <- context_tree(trajectories)
tree
#> <transitiontrees>  145 nodes, depth <= 5, 3 states  [unpruned]
#>   alphabet : Active, Average, Disengaged
#>   fit on   : 136 sequences, 1870 observations
#>   smoothing: floor(ymin=0.001, rule=interpolate)   min_count = 5
#> (start)   n=1870   -> Average (0.43)
#> |-- Active    n=658    -> Active (0.70)
#> |   |-- Active    n=433    -> Active (0.79)
#> |   |   |-- Active    n=316    -> Active (0.84)
#> |   |   |   |-- Active    n=240    -> Active (0.87)
#> |   |   |   |   |-- Active    n=192    -> Active (0.87)
#> |   |   |   |   |-- Average   n=17     -> Active (0.76)
#> |   |   |   |   `-- Disengaged  n=7      -> Active (0.71)
#> |   |   |   |-- Average   n=37     -> Active (0.59)
#> |   |   |   |   |-- Active    n=12     -> Active (0.83)
#> |   |   |   |   `-- Average   n=18     -> Active (0.50)
#> |   |   |   `-- Disengaged  n=10     -> Active (0.90)
#> |   |   |       `-- Active    n=5      -> Active (0.80)
#> |   |   |-- Average   n=70     -> Active (0.53)
#> |   |   |   |-- Active    n=22     -> Active (0.55)
#> |   |   |   |   |-- Active    n=11     -> Active (0.64)
#> |   |   |   |   `-- Average   n=10     -> Average (0.50)
#> |   |   |   `-- Average   n=37     -> Active (0.49)
#> |   |   |       |-- Active    n=15     -> Active (0.53)
#> |   |   |       |-- Average   n=15     -> Active (0.47)
#> |   |   |       `-- Disengaged  n=7      -> Average (0.57)
#> |   |   `-- Disengaged  n=15     -> Active (0.67)
#> |   |       |-- Active    n=5      -> Active (1.00)
#> |   |       `-- Average   n=7      -> Average (0.57)
#> |   |-- Average   n=144    -> Active (0.50)
#> |   |   |-- Active    n=53     -> Average (0.55) 
#> ... 119 more nodes (use as.data.frame(x) or summary(x))

The banner is the whole model at a glance: how many contexts it holds, the alphabet, how many sequences and observations it was fit on, and — at the bottom — the next state predicted from no history (the (start) row) followed by the tree of deeper contexts. With the defaults the tree grows to depth 5, so it is large and over-grown; the next step trims it.

Prune the tree

pruned <- prune_tree(tree)
pruned
#> <transitiontrees>  32 nodes, depth <= 5, 3 states  [pruned]
#>   alphabet : Active, Average, Disengaged
#>   fit on   : 136 sequences, 1870 observations
#>   smoothing: floor(ymin=0.001, rule=interpolate)   min_count = 5
#>   pruned by: G2   alpha = 0.05
#> (start)   n=1870   -> Average (0.43)
#> |-- Active    n=658    -> Active (0.70)
#> |   |-- Active    n=433    -> Active (0.79)
#> |   |   |-- Active    n=316    -> Active (0.84)
#> |   |   |   `-- Average   n=37     -> Active (0.59)
#> |   |   `-- Average   n=70     -> Active (0.53)
#> |   `-- Average   n=144    -> Active (0.50)
#> |       `-- Disengaged  n=12     -> Average (0.83)
#> |-- Average   n=751    -> Average (0.61)
#> |   |-- Active    n=160    -> Average (0.52)
#> |   |   |-- Active    n=74     -> Average (0.55)
#> |   |   |   `-- Active    n=40     -> Active (0.53)
#> |   |   |       `-- Average   n=13     -> Active (0.77)
#> |   |   `-- Disengaged  n=10     -> Average (0.50)
#> |   |-- Average   n=419    -> Average (0.68)
#> |   |   |-- Active    n=80     -> Average (0.57)
#> |   |   |   `-- Active    n=38     -> Average (0.50)
#> |   |   |       `-- Active    n=13     -> Active (0.61)
#> |   |   `-- Average   n=248    -> Average (0.72)
#> |   |       |-- Average   n=148    -> Average (0.74)
#> |   |       |   `-- Active    n=24     -> Average (0.71)
#> |   |       `-- Disengaged  n=37     -> Average (0.70)
#> |   `-- Disengaged  n=122    -> Average (0.52)
#> |       `-- Disengaged  n=31     -> Average (0.71)
#> `-- Disengaged  n=325    -> Disengaged (0.48)
#>     |-- Active    n=23     -> Active (0.39) 
#> ... 6 more nodes (use as.data.frame(x) or summary(x))

The default fit above grows all the way to depth 5 and keeps deep contexts the data barely supports (over-fitting). Pruning keeps a longer history only when it predicts better than its shorter version, so the tree gets much smaller — the banner now reads pruned by: G2. The rest of the vignette uses this pruned tree.

Draw the tree

plot(pruned)

The default picture: a left-to-right tree. Node size is how often a context occurs, branch thickness is the volume flowing down it, and colour is the most recent state. The thick branches are the common engagement patterns; the thin twigs are the rarer contexts pruning kept on evidence.

Most common pathways

common_pathways(pruned)

The trajectories the data simply contains the most copies of, ranked by count. likely_next and next_probability say what tends to come after each — the everyday flow of engagement.

Most divergent pathways

divergent_pathways(pruned)
plot_divergence(pruned)

These are the contexts where knowing the longer history changes the prediction most (the divergence column, drawn as the lollipop). Orange points mark pathways where the extra history actually flips which state is most likely — the genuinely informative histories, as opposed to the merely common ones above.

Sharpest pathways

sharp_pathways(pruned)

Ranked by next_probability: the contexts whose next state is most nearly certain. High sharpness with a high count is a dependable rule; high sharpness on a small count is just chance, and the bootstrap section below separates the two.

Per-context diagnostics

tree_dependence(pruned)

The information-theoretic view of each context: its divergence, its entropy (how uncertain its next state is), the parent’s entropy_before, and entropy_drop — how much uncertainty the context removes. A large entropy_drop with changes_prediction = TRUE is the most valuable kind of context: it both sharpens and redirects.

Pathway × next-state heatmap

plot_pathways(pruned)

The same pathways as a grid: each row a context, each column a next state, each cell P(next | context). The bright cells are the strong rules; a > prefix marks a context whose history flips the most likely next state.

Goodness of fit

model_fit(pruned)

The standard scalars in one row. perplexity is the readable one — the effective number of equally-likely next states; against a uniform baseline of three states, the tree’s value shows how much the recent history narrows things down. AIC/BIC penalise the node count and let you weigh two candidate trees.

Reliability under resampling

boot <- bootstrap_pathways(pruned)
boot
#> <transitiontrees_bootstrap>  1000 resamples
#>   stability  : count in [0.50, 1.50] x observed, p < 0.05
#>   informative: G^2 > qchisq(0.95, df=k-1) = 5.99, threshold 0.80
#>   pathways   : 32 total, 27 stable, 17 informative, 16 both
#> 
#> top pathways (stable + informative first):
#>                                pathway depth count p_stability stability_rate
#>                                Average     1   751       0.001          1.000
#>                                 Active     1   658       0.001          1.000
#>                       Active -> Active     2   433       0.001          1.000
#>                             Disengaged     1   325       0.001          1.000
#>                      Active -> Average     2   160       0.001          1.000
#>                      Average -> Active     2   144       0.001          1.000
#>                  Disengaged -> Average     2   122       0.001          1.000
#>           Active -> Average -> Average     3    80       0.001          1.000
#>            Average -> Active -> Active     3    70       0.001          1.000
#>  Average -> Active -> Active -> Active     4    37       0.002          0.999
#>  stable informative_rate informative mean_G2 ci_G2_lo ci_G2_hi
#>    TRUE            1.000        TRUE 119.761   76.381  171.708
#>    TRUE            1.000        TRUE 319.894  247.812  397.557
#>    TRUE            0.994        TRUE  18.241    7.875   30.772
#>    TRUE            1.000        TRUE 181.559  111.364  261.946
#>    TRUE            0.997        TRUE  28.844   12.087   50.532
#>    TRUE            1.000        TRUE  30.912   12.900   53.404
#>    TRUE            1.000        TRUE  31.807   14.086   52.481
#>    TRUE            0.858        TRUE  12.685    2.329   26.726
#>    TRUE            0.995        TRUE  24.069    9.891   43.970
#>    TRUE            0.844        TRUE  13.486    2.331   31.458
#> # ... 22 more pathways (use summary(x) for full table)
plot(boot)

The bootstrap refits the tree on resampled learners and asks which pathways survive reproducibly. The forest plot shows each pathway’s bootstrap interval against the significance line; a bar entirely to the right is reproducibly informative. The pathways that are both stable and informative are the ones worth building a claim on.

Other views of the same tree

plot(pruned, style = "icicle")

plot(pruned, style = "dendrogram")

The same pruned tree as a sunburst and as a radial dendrogram — the encodings (size = count, fill = state, depth = rings or radius) are identical, only the layout changes. There is also plot(pruned, style = "interactive") for a draggable version.
